title: Priority Queues & Heaps
description: Heaps and Priority Queues in Python. Leveraging the built-in heapq module to implement binary heaps for O(log N) elements sorting.

sections.title: Min-Heap
sections.description: Python's heapq module provides binary heap algorithms built on top of standard lists. The minimum element is always stored at index 0 (min-heap invariant).
sections.codeBlock:
import heapq

heap = []

# Push elements - O(log N) time complexity
heapq.heappush(heap, 40)
heapq.heappush(heap, 10)
heapq.heappush(heap, 30)
heapq.heappush(heap, 15)

# Peek smallest element - O(1) time complexity
print(heap[0])         # 10

# Pop smallest element - O(log N) time complexity
print(heapq.heappop(heap)) # 10
print(heapq.heappop(heap)) # 15

sections.title: Max-Heap
sections.description: Python does not have a built-in max-heap implementation, but you can easily simulate one by multiplying element values by -1 during insertion and popping.
sections.codeBlock:
import heapq

max_heap = []

# Push element multiplied by -1
heapq.heappush(max_heap, -40)
heapq.heappush(max_heap, -10)
heapq.heappush(max_heap, -30)

# Retrieve elements (multiply by -1 again)
print(-heapq.heappop(max_heap)) # 40 (largest)
print(-heapq.heappop(max_heap)) # 30

details:
Binary Heap Mechanics: A binary heap is a complete binary tree where the key of any node is smaller (in a min-heap) or larger (in a max-heap) than its children. Python's heapq module uses a zero-based flat list representation of this tree. Under the hood, for any node at index i, its left child is at index 2*i + 1, and its right child is at 2*i + 2. This layout avoids pointer overhead completely.

Performance & Use Cases: Pushing and popping elements take O(log N) time, while heapifying a raw list takes O(N) linear time using heapq.heapify(). Heaps are the backing data structure for Priority Queues and are heavily used in greedy scheduling, Huffman coding, Dijkstra's shortest path algorithm, and finding the k-largest or k-smallest elements in large data streams.
